questions.question: Explain the difference between user-level threads and kernel-level threads. Mention two advantages of each approach.
keyPoints: User-level threads are managed entirely in user space without kernel involvement
Kernel-level threads are directly supported by the operating system
User-level threads: fast context switching, flexible scheduling, OS independence
Kernel-level threads: can utilize multiple processors, kernel can schedule threads independently
Hybrid approaches combine both advantages

questions.question: What is a race condition? How can it be prevented using mutex locks?
keyPoints: Race condition occurs when multiple threads access/modify shared data concurrently
Result depends on timing of thread execution - unpredictable behavior
Mutex ensures only one thread can access critical section at a time
pthread_mutex_lock() before and pthread_mutex_unlock() after critical section
Proper lock acquisition and release pattern prevents race conditions

questions.question: Write and explain the syntax of pthread_create() function in POSIX threads.
keyPoints: Function signature: int pthread_create(pthread_t *thread, const pthread_attr_t *attr, void *(*start_routine)(void*), void *arg)
First parameter: pointer to pthread_t to store thread ID
Second parameter: thread attributes (NULL for default attributes)
Third parameter: function pointer for thread start routine
Fourth parameter: argument to pass to start routine
Returns 0 on success, error code on failure

questions.question: Describe the purpose of condition variables in thread synchronization. When should condition variables be used?
keyPoints: Condition variables allow threads to wait for specific conditions to become true
Used with mutexes for efficient thread coordination
Typical pattern: lock mutex, check condition, wait (releases lock), signal/broadcast when condition changes
Used in producer-consumer, reader-writer, and other synchronization scenarios
Solves the problem of busy-waiting by allowing threads to sleep

questions.question: What is thread-specific data (TSD)? How is it implemented in Pthreads?
keyPoints: TSD allows each thread to have its own copy of global or static variables
pthread_key_create() creates a key for thread-specific data
pthread_setspecific() stores data associated with a key for calling thread
pthread_getspecific() retrieves thread-specific data for the calling thread
Useful for maintaining per-thread state without using global variables

questions.question: Explain the concept of thread cancellation in POSIX threads. What are the different cancellation types?
keyPoints: pthread_cancel() sends cancellation request to target thread
Deferred cancellation: cancellation request checked at cancellation points (default type)
Asynchronous cancellation: cancellation can occur immediately at any point
Disabled cancellation: thread can protect critical operations using pthread_setcancelstate()
Cleanup handlers using pthread_cleanup_push() and pthread_cleanup_pop()

questions.question: Compare thread libraries in Windows and POSIX environments. Mention at least three differences.
keyPoints: Windows uses CreateThread(), POSIX uses pthread_create()
Windows handles are kernel objects, Pthreads use portable thread IDs
Windows provides WaitForSingleObject(), POSIX uses pthread_join()
Mutex initialization differs: InitializeCriticalSection() vs pthread_mutex_init()
POSIX is standardized and portable; Windows threads are platform-specific

questions.question: Write a complete C program using Pthreads to demonstrate producer-consumer problem with a bounded buffer. Include proper synchronization using mutex and condition variables.
keyPoints: Define shared buffer and buffer size
Initialize mutex and condition variables
Create producer thread(s) that add items to buffer
Create consumer thread(s) that remove items from buffer
Use pthread_cond_signal() when buffer has space
Use pthread_cond_signal() when buffer has items
Proper error handling for all pthread functions
Show correct output demonstrating synchronization

questions.question: Explain the various thread scheduling algorithms. How does multi-processor scheduling differ from single-processor scheduling?
keyPoints: First-Come-First-Served (FCFS): jobs scheduled in arrival order
Shortest Job First (SJF): minimizes average waiting time
Priority Scheduling: higher priority jobs run first
Round Robin: time-slicing for fair sharing
Multi-processor scheduling faces additional challenges: load balancing, processor affinity, cache coherence
Symmetric multiprocessing (SMP): each processor runs scheduler independently
NUMA considerations in modern multi-core systems
Thread migration and its impact on performance

questions.question: What are the different threading models? Explain one-to-one, many-to-one, and many-to-many models with their advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: One-to-One model: each user thread maps to one kernel thread - good parallelism but limited scalability
Many-to-One model: multiple user threads map to one kernel thread - efficient but no true parallelism
Many-to-Many model: many user threads to many kernel threads - combines advantages of both
Java uses one-to-one model
Linux and Windows use one-to-one models
Solaris supports many-to-many model
Thread pooling as alternative approach

questions.question: Write a Java program demonstrating thread creation using both Thread class and Runnable interface. Explain the difference between these two approaches.
keyPoints: Create thread by extending Thread class and overriding run() method
Create thread by implementing Runnable interface and passing to Thread constructor
Runnable approach preferred for Java due to single inheritance limitation
Thread class provides additional methods like getName(), setPriority()
Demonstrate starting thread with start() method vs directly calling run()
Thread lifecycle states: NEW, RUNNABLE, BLOCKED, WAITING, TIMED_WAITING, TERMINATED
